revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:品官]]
'''Hinkan''' (品官) refers to a general term for {{ill|official positions|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/official positions|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452125}} within the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} that do not belong to the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier System of Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} hierarchy of each {{ill|Executive (government)|ja|官司|lt=government office|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Executive (government)|1=kanshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452091}}.
In the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} of China, a group of official positions also called "Hinkan" existed, referring to all posts Ranked {{ill|Junior Ninth Rank|ja|従九品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Ninth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or higher in the {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Court Ranks of Japan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. These correspond to Japan's {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikiji Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 職事|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11611227}}. While the term "Hinkan" in Japan derives from its Chinese counterpart, its meaning is entirely different. (However, there is a theory that "Hinkan" was also used in Japan with the meaning of Shikiji Officials.)
In Japan, Hinkan positions were organizationally affiliated with certain government offices but formed separate bureaus ({{ill|Bekkyoku|ja|別局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bekkyoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) with highly specialized functions. They were distinct from the Four-Tier Officials and were assigned Ranks according to the {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|qid=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system|1=List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles}} in the {{ill|Kan-i-ryō|ja|官位令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-i-ryō|1=Official Rank Ordinance|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97341948}}. Representative examples of Hinkan include: - {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2948088}}, {{ill|Naiki|ja|内記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naiki|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kenmotsu|ja|監物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmotsu|qq=}} under the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|qid=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center}}. - {{ill|Hanza|ja|判事 (律令制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanza|qq=}} under the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qid=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)}}.
In China's Ritsuryō system, separate bureaus also existed, but they operated under the principle of the {{ill|Three Judge System|ja|三判制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Judge System|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where the "Chief Official" (長官) and "Deputy Official" (次官) supervised a "Judge" (判官) overseeing the bureau. Additionally, supervision occurred under the {{ill|Inspection System (Kenkō-sei)|ja|検勾制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inspection System (Kenkō-sei)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In contrast, no such hierarchical relationship existed between Japan's Four-Tier Officials and Hinkan. This discrepancy arose because Japan's Ritsuryō system allowed individual ministries (省), offices (職), bureaus (司), and agencies (寮) to maintain a degree of independence. Furthermore, independent offices (e.g., Kenmotsu and Hanza) that predated the Ritsuryō system were forcibly incorporated into specific ministries while retaining their traditional autonomy. As a result, many Hinkan positions were concentrated in the Nakatsukasashō, which oversaw general administrative matters of the court.
== References == * {{ill|Tatsuya Torio|ja|虎尾達哉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuya Torio|1=Tatsuya Torao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q105764051}}, "Hinkan," in *National History Dictionary Vol. 12* (Kikugawa Kōbunkan, 1991), ISBN 978-4-642-00512-8. * Kiyoharu Ōkuma, "Hinkan," in *Japanese Historical Encyclopedia Vol. 6* (Heibonsha, 1994), ISBN 978-4-582-13106-2.
{{translated page|ja|品官|version=82195630|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:品官]]
<!--Hinkan--><!--Hinkan--><!--Hinkan-->
